title: Prophet Zechariah, The 40 Martyrs of Persia, St. Pambo & St. Sophia of Thrace
narrative:
title": "Prophet Zechariah, The 40 Martyrs of Persia, St. Pambo & St. Sophia of Thrace

narrative": "The Prophet Zechariah, the Forty Martyrs of Persia, Abba Pambo of Egypt, and St. Sophia of Thrace

On the fifteenth day of Yekatit, the holy Church holds together four witnesses whose lives illuminate the prophetic, the martyrological, the monastic, and the domestic vocations as four ways of the same single-hearted orientation toward God. The Prophet Zechariah, son of Berechiah, stands first: the post-exilic visionary whose eight visions in a single night revealed to him the restoration of Jerusalem, the cleansing of the high priest, the coming of the Branch, and the entry of the peaceful king on a donkey — images that the New Testament writers received as direct prophecies of the Passion and the Resurrection of Christ. His martyrdom in the Temple, slain between the sanctuary and the altar, places him in the company of those whose prophetic word cost them their lives in the very place they had consecrated to God.

The Forty Martyrs of Persia are the great anonymous company of this day — Christians put to death during one of the periodic persecutions of the Persian Empire, which fell with particular ferocity upon those who had accepted the religion of the Roman enemy. Persian Christian martyrdom has a distinctive character in the Synaxarium tradition: it is often the martyrdom of entire communities, of priests and deacons and laypeople together, of families and congregations rather than of solitary individuals, and this communal quality is itself a witness to the nature of the Church as a body in which each member suffers with every other. The forty of Persia stand in the tradition of the forty at Sebaste, holding the same number of completeness and sacrifice.

Abba Pambo of Egypt is one of the great figures of the early desert who has never quite received the attention his stature deserves. A disciple of Antony and a teacher of Ammonius, he was consulted by the most learned theologians of his age, including the great Melania the Elder who brought gold to him as a gift; he weighed it without looking up from his work and said he would trust God to repay her. He spent nineteen years in meditation on a single verse of Scripture — I will take heed to my ways, that I sin not with my tongue — and was asked at the end of those years whether he had mastered the verse; he replied that he had barely made a beginning. This story is preserved in the Apophthegmata Patrum as the paradigm of monastic humility and the long patience required for genuine spiritual progress.

Saint Sophia of Thrace is the figure of discovery in this day's assembly: a woman who had lived a life of great holiness in the region of Thrace and who, at a time of persecution, chose to remain at her place of prayer rather than flee, and was martyred there. The tradition records almost nothing about her biography except the quality of her death — peaceful, unresisting, oriented entirely toward God rather than toward the circumstances of her dying. The Ethiopian Synaxarium preserves her memory as a witness to what the Greek fathers called apatheia, that freedom from agitation in the face of the worst that the world can do which is the fruit of long prayer and total trust in the divine providence. Zechariah the visionary, the anonymous forty of Persia, Pambo who spent nineteen years on one verse, Sophia who died quietly in Thrace: four lives, four ways of the one unreserved gift of the self to God. Amen.
